title: Session 12: The Exit Boundary $L^*(t)$ — Numerical Examples

markdown.headingText: Session 12: The Exit Boundary $L^*(t)$ — Numerical Examples
markdown.markdown:
| | |
|---|---|
| **Unit** | 3 — Decision and Application |
| **Book Chapter** | 6 (sections 6.5–6.7) |
| **Slide source** | Reuse from Class 13 + ~5 new |
| **Slides needed** | ~5 new + adapt ~6 from Class 13 |
| **Track** | Common core (both tracks) |

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this session, students will be able to:

1. **Read** an exit boundary $L^*(t)$ chart and identify the hold region vs. exit region.
2. **Explain** why $L^*(t)$ rises as $t \to T$ (the boundary terminal-time effect).
3. **Compute** the optimal exit decision for a given (current $L$, remaining $T$) pair using the calibrated boundary.
4. **Identify** how the exit boundary differs across asset classes (PE buyout vs. infrastructure vs. private credit).
5. **Apply** the exit boundary to one of the major historical episodes (GFC 2009, COVID 2020) and identify what GE-LAV would have recommended.

## Pre-Class Assignment

- **Read:** Book Chapter 6, sections 6.5–6.7 (~8 pages)
- **Try:** Sketch on paper what you think the exit boundary looks like — does $L^*$ rise or fall as $t$ increases? Why?

## In-Class Outline (75 minutes)

| Time | Segment | Format |
|---|---|---|
| 0:00–0:10 | Recap Session 11 · Set up today's numerical work | Lecture |
| 0:10–0:25 | The exit boundary chart — reading the figure | Lecture |
| 0:25–0:40 | Why $L^*(t)$ has the shape it does | Lecture + chart deep-dive |
| 0:40–0:55 | Sensitivity to OU parameters and asset class | Lecture |
| 0:55–1:10 | Historical application: GFC, COVID, rate shock | Case + discussion |
| 1:10–1:15 | Worked example: should this LP exit? | Live problem |

## Slide Content Briefs (New Slides Needed)

### Slide 12.1 — Title and Today's Goal

- **Title:** "The Exit Boundary $L^*(t)$ in Practice"
- **Tag line:** "Yesterday: the structure. Today: the numbers."
- **Speaker notes:** Today is operational. Students should leave able to look at a position and identify the exit recommendation.

### Slide 12.2 — The Exit Boundary Chart (Anchor Visual)

- **Title:** "The GE-LAV exit boundary"
- **Visual:** Master chart showing $L^*(t)$ as a function of remaining time-to-maturity $T - t$
- **Key features to annotate:**
  - X-axis: years remaining (0 to 15)
  - Y-axis: critical liquidity state $L^*$
  - Boundary curve: rises from approximately $L^* = -2.0$ at $T - t = 10$+ years, up to $L^* \approx -0.5$ at $T - t = 1$ year, to $L^* \approx 0$ near maturity
  - **Hold region:** Above the curve
  - **Exit region:** Below the curve
- **Calibrated benchmark numbers (table):**

| Remaining T | $L^*$ |
|---|---|
| 10 yr | −1.8 |
| 7 yr | −1.5 |
| 5 yr | −1.2 |
| 3 yr | −0.8 |
| 1 yr | −0.5 |
| 0.5 yr | −0.2 |

- **Speaker notes:** This chart is the operational tool. Spend 10+ minutes on it.

### Slide 12.3 — Why $L^*(t)$ Rises as $t \to T$

- **Title:** "The shape of $L^*(t)$, intuitively"
- **Three reasons:**
  1. **Less time for mean reversion:** Near maturity, even small stress can't be recovered before forced exit at terminal date
  2. **Smaller Jensen benefit:** Jensen bias scales with $T$ — less to gain by holding short-term
  3. **Asymmetric payoff:** Distress risk dominates upside as horizon shrinks
- **Visual:** Three small annotations on the master chart
- **Speaker notes:** Connect to Black-Scholes intuition — option time value decays as expiry approaches. Same principle.

### Slide 12.4 — Asset Class Differences

- **Title:** "Exit boundaries vary by asset class"
- **Table or overlaid chart:**

| Asset Class | $L^*$ at 5 years remaining |
|---|---|
| PE Buyout | −1.2 |
| Growth Equity | −1.1 |
| Real Estate | −1.4 |
| Infrastructure | −1.6 |
| Private Credit | −0.9 |

- **Explanation of pattern:**
  - **Infrastructure** has the most negative $L^*$ → most willing to hold through stress (long mean-reversion runway, stable cash flows)
  - **Private credit** has the least negative $L^*$ → quickest to exit (short duration, less Jensen benefit to hold)
- **Speaker notes:** Asset class matters for the boundary. This is why portfolio-level exit policy needs asset-class differentiation.

### Slide 12.5 — Calibrated Exit Boundary Performance (Class 13 Reuse)

- **Title:** "Historical performance of the GE-LAV exit rule"
- **Setup:** "If LPs had applied the GE-LAV exit boundary 1990–2024, what would have happened?"
- **Results table (from Class 13 reuse):**

| Episode | Date | Standard LP Outcome | GE-LAV-Optimal Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| LTCM | 1998 | −12% capital loss on PE secondaries | Hold; no loss |
| Dot-com | 2001–2002 | −18% on early VC secondaries | Partial exit @ −0.5σ → −8% |
| GFC | 2008–2010 | −35% on PE secondaries | Exit signal triggered → 0% to −10% |
| COVID | 2020 | −15% briefly | Hold; recovered by Q3 |
| Rate shock | 2022 | −18% PE, −22% RE | Partial exit signal in RE |

- **Speaker notes:** This is one of the most empirically powerful slides in the course. GE-LAV would have improved LP outcomes in 3 of 5 major episodes.

## Existing Slides to Reuse from Class 13

From Deck 3, Class 13 ("PE Return Decomposition: Five-Component Attribution"), pull approximately 6 slides:

- Slide showing the $L^*(t)$ master chart (this is the anchor visual)
- Slide showing historical performance of the exit rule
- Slide showing sensitivity to OU parameters (κ, σ variations)
- Slide showing asset class comparison
- Slide showing the smooth pasting visual
- One slide showing a numerical example

**Adaptation:** Class 13 was titled "PE Return Decomposition" but contains substantial exit-boundary content. Repurpose these slides as the bulk of Session 12.

## Discussion Questions

1. CalPERS publicly disclosed 2009 PE secondary sales at 25% discount. Using the exit boundary chart, would GE-LAV have recommended this sale? What information beyond the chart would CalPERS have needed?
2. The exit boundary $L^* \approx -0.5$ at 1-year horizon. Most fund extensions are 1-2 years. Does the boundary suggest fund extensions should be granted (hold) or refused (force exit)? Why?
3. If your investment policy statement (IPS) says "no secondary sales except in distress," is that consistent with GE-LAV-optimal behavior? When would it diverge?

## Worked Numerical Example: Should This LP Exit?

**Scenario:** It is Q4 2024. An insurance company holds a 9-year-old infrastructure fund position. Reported NAV = $200M. Position has 4 years remaining until natural liquidation.

**Current conditions:**
- Estimated $L_t = -0.7$ (moderately stressed; secondary discounts around 18%)
- Asset class: infrastructure
- Remaining horizon: 4 years
- Secondary market bid: 22% discount → $156M cash today
- Solvency II capital charge if held: increases by ~3% of NAV
- Solvency II capital charge if sold: decreases by ~5% of NAV (cash is treated favorably)

**Step 1: What does the exit boundary say?**

For infrastructure at 4 years remaining: $L^* \approx -1.5$

Current $L_t = -0.7$ vs. $L^* = -1.5$

**$L_t > L^*$, so the GE-LAV recommendation is HOLD.**

**Step 2: But Solvency II is forcing pressure**

The capital charge differential (3% + 5% = 8% effective swing) creates a regulatory wedge that GE-LAV doesn't directly model. If the insurer would be capital-constrained by holding:
- Hold cost: 8% of $200M = $16M of capital tied up
- Sell cost: 22% discount = $44M of value sacrificed

**Net:** Selling sacrifices $44M − $16M = $28M of value vs. holding through.

**Step 3: GE-LAV expected value of holding**

Under OU dynamics, $E[L_{t+4}] = \bar{L} + (L_t - \bar{L}) e^{-4\kappa} = 1.0 + (-0.7 - 1.0) e^{-1.8} = 1.0 + (-1.7)(0.165) = 0.72$

The expected liquidity state in 4 years is +0.72, well above current. The Jensen bias for 4-year infrastructure: $B(4) = 0.18\% \times 4 = 0.72\%$

Expected hold value: $\$200M \times (1 + 0.0072) \approx \$201.4M$, plus mean-reversion gain (state goes from −0.7 to +0.7 generates ~5–8% NAV uplift via lower discount rates).

**Conclusion:** Pure GE-LAV says hold. Hybrid view (GE-LAV + Solvency II): probably still hold, but the case is closer than a non-regulatory LP would face.

**Teaching note:** This is a realistic decision insurance allocators face quarterly. The framework is more useful for *defending* the hold decision to a board than for making a clear binary call.

## Common Student Confusions (Instructor Notes)

- **"The boundary is so deep ($L^* \approx -1.5$). When would anyone actually trigger exit?"** GFC was −2.0+ at peak distress. COVID briefly touched −1.0. Boundary is conservative on purpose — exit is value-destroying unless conditions are extreme.
- **"What if my secondary market price is below GE-LAV equilibrium?"** Then the secondary market is offering you a *bad* deal, and you should hold. The exit boundary uses the secondary price as the alternative — if that price is too low, the boundary correctly says hold.
- **"Does the boundary depend on my personal $\gamma$ (risk aversion)?"** Slightly. Higher $\gamma$ → exit more readily (less willing to bear hold risk). But the boundary moves only modestly across reasonable $\gamma$ values.
- **"What if I disagree with the OU calibration?"** That's the right question. Robustness checks (using bootstrap CI on $\kappa, \sigma, \bar{L}$) show the boundary is robust to ±20% parameter variation. Anything beyond that, recalibrate.

## What to Expect Next Session

Session 13 covers **liquidity traps and the historical performance** of the GE-LAV exit rule across multiple episodes. We'll examine:

- The 2008–2010 GFC in detail (the worst-case test of the framework)
- COVID 2020 (sharp shock, fast recovery — does GE-LAV handle this?)
- Rate shock 2022 (slow-burn vs. fast crisis)
- The Pigouvian exit tax (preview — full treatment Session 24)

**Reading:** Book Chapter 6, sections 6.7–6.10 (~10 pages).
